hero.tagline: Standard $25,000 cap with jurisdiction varying by county.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Filing fee
at_a_glance.detail: Fees are set by local courts

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in Circuit Court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If a claim exceeds the $25,000 cap, you must waive the excess to file in General Sessions Court; splitting one dispute into multiple claims is not allowed.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Tennessee allows 6 years to file for breach of a written contract and 6 years for oral contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $20,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $5,000, and they failed to repay.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness testimony.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned $3,000 to a colleague who promised to repay in 3 months but hasn't.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Document the loan terms and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $10,000 and missed payments.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the original note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $8,000 worth of consulting services, but the client hasn't paid the invoice.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Include the invoice, service agreement, and communication records.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $15,000 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,500 in unpaid rent over the past three months.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid with a $1,200 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: Provide the original check, bank notice of insufficient funds, and any related correspondence.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer liability for defaulted loan
claims.example: You co-signed a $7,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the loan agreement and evidence of default.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Tennessee provides a 1-year statute of limitations for consumer protection claims.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $500 appliance that stopped working within a month.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Keep the purchase receipt and any repair records.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages + attorney's fees; possible treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $300 but left your home dirty.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Document the service agreement and issues encountered.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages + attorney's fees; possible treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $1,500 for repairs that didn't fix the problem.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Retain repair invoices and any expert evaluations.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages + attorney's fees; possible treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory work by a contractor
claims.example: A contractor was paid $10,000 for a renovation but left the job incomplete.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Gather the contract, payment records, and photos of unfinished work.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages + attorney's fees; possible treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods/services
claims.example: You paid $2,000 for catering services, but the vendor never showed up.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Provide the service agreement and proof of payment.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages + attorney's fees; possible treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month subscription, but charges continued for 3 months.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Keep records of cancellation and subsequent charges.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages + attorney's fees; possible treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $8,000 that broke down within a week.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Retain the sales contract and any repair assessments.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages + attorney's fees; possible treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: False advertising leading to a purchase
claims.example: A store advertised a laptop for $1,000 with features it didn't have.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Save the advertisement and purchase receipt.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages + attorney's fees; possible treble damages for willful violations.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Tennessee allows 3 years to file for property damage claims.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver caused $5,000 damage to your car in an accident.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Obtain the police report and repair estimates.

claims.name: Neighbor's actions damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell and caused $2,000 damage to your fence.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Take photos and gather repair quotes.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left $3,500 worth of damage upon moving out.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Document the damage with photos and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog chewed up your $1,200 outdoor furniture.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from incident. Collect evidence of the damage and any related expenses.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: A dry cleaner ruined your $500 suit.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Keep the receipt and photos of the damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Tennessee provides specific statutes governing landlord-tenant relationships, including security deposits and habitability standards.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,000 security deposit without explanation.
claims.notes: Landlords must return the deposit or provide an itemized list of deductions within 30 days.

claims.name: No itemization of security deposit deductions
claims.example: Landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without itemizing damages.
claims.notes: Landlords are required to provide an itemized list of deductions within 30 days.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had no heat during winter, and the landlord refused to fix it.
claims.notes: Landlords must maintain habitable conditions; tenants can seek damages for violations.

claims.name: Landlord retained tenant's personal property
claims.example: After eviction, the landlord kept your $2,000 worth of belongings.
claims.notes: Landlords must follow proper procedures for handling abandoned property.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing access to your home.
claims.notes: Self-help evictions are illegal; tenants can seek damages for unlawful eviction.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but the landlord didn't return the last month's rent after early termination.
claims.notes: Landlords must return prepaid rent if the lease is terminated early without cause.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed two months' rent totaling $1,800.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You fixed a $300 plumbing issue and deducted it from rent after the landlord's inaction.
claims.notes: Tenants may repair and deduct if the landlord fails to address issues after notice.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Tennessee mandates timely payment of wages and provides remedies for violations.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but not paid.
claims.notes: Employers must pay wages promptly; employees can seek unpaid wages through legal action.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resignation, your employer didn't provide your last paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: Final wages are due on the next regular payday; withholding may lead to penalties.

claims.name: Unpaid sales commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in commissions that your employer hasn't paid.
claims.notes: Commissions are considered wages and must be paid accordingly.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but weren't compensated at the overtime rate.
claims.notes: Overtime must be paid as required by law; violations can result in penalties.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Tennessee allows 1 year to file for personal injury claims.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped in a store and sustained a $2,000 medical bill.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from injury. Collect medical records and incident reports.

claims.name: Damage to loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,000 camera, which they returned broken.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Document the condition before and after lending.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your $500 lawnmower without permission.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. File a police report and gather evidence of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific claims
claim_categories.blurb: Tennessee provides remedies for disputes involving vehicle repairs and towing.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Keep repair invoices and any expert evaluations.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages + attorney's fees; possible treble damages for willful violations.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, costing you $300.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Obtain towing records and photos of the parking area.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages + attorney's fees; possible treble damages for willful violations.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction proceedings are handled separately and are not within the jurisdiction of General Sessions Court.
Disputes involving the title to real estate exceed the jurisdiction of General Sessions Court.
Defamation claims are complex and typically exceed the jurisdictional limits of General Sessions Court.
Professional malpractice claims require detailed evidence and are beyond the scope of General Sessions Court.
Matters such as divorce, child custody, and probate are handled by specialized courts.
Claims involving federal law, such as bankruptcy or patents, are outside the jurisdiction of state courts.
Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the jurisdictional limits of General Sessions Court.
General Sessions Court cannot grant equitable remedies like injunctions or specific performance.
Suits against the federal government must be filed in federal court.
Workers' compensation claims are handled by specialized administrative courts.
Disputes subject to mandatory arbitration clauses are typically resolved outside of court.
Claims against deceased individuals must be filed against their estate in probate court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in General Sessions Court; however, statutory penalties may apply in certain cases.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney's fees may be recoverable in cases involving the Tennessee Consumer Protection Act or bad checks.

filing_options: Any county where ONE defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer, leading to delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability depends on the county; check with the local court clerk.

forms.name: Request to Postpone Filing Fee and Order

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may include service fees; confirm with the local court.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can facilitate settlement and is advisable.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: A 30-day written notice is required before pursuing civil penalties for a bad check.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific demand requirement, but written notice can strengthen the claim.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days when withholding a security deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing notice required under the Tennessee Consumer Protection Act.

forms.name: Civil Warrant
forms.description: Initiates a civil lawsuit in General Sessions Court.

forms.name: Summons
forms.description: Notifies the defendant of the lawsuit and court date.

forms.name: Affidavit of Complaint
forms.description: Details the plaintiff's claims against the defendant.

forms.name: Counterclaim
forms.description: Allows the defendant to file a claim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Motion to Dismiss
forms.description: Requests the court to dismiss the case for specific reasons.

forms.name: Request for Continuance
forms.description: Asks the court to reschedule the hearing date.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Indicates that the judgment has been paid in full.

forms.name: Writ of Possession
forms.description: Orders the sheriff to remove a tenant and return possession to the landlord.

forms.name: Writ of Execution
forms.description: Authorizes the seizure of the defendant's property to satisfy a judgment.

forms.name: Garnishment
forms.description: Directs an employer to withhold wages to satisfy a judgment.

forms.name: Appeal Bond
forms.description: Filed to appeal a General Sessions Court decision to Circuit Court.

forms.name: Pauper's Oath in Lieu of Appeal Bond
forms.description: Allows an indigent party to appeal without posting a bond.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: Clerk sends the documents via certified mail to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed individual delivers the documents to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Tennessee's long-arm statute and the laws of the state where the defendant resides.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, you may request alternative methods of service from the court.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear in court on the scheduled date.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim and amount of damages.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: A motion to set aside must be filed within 10 days; if denied, an appeal must be filed within 10 days of the denial.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may represent themselves or be represented by an attorney.
hearing.format: Bench trial, typically lasting 15-30 minutes per case.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Parties must arrange for their own interpreters; some courts may provide assistance upon request.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within 2 weeks
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some counties offer mediation services; check with the local court clerk.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal results in a new trial in Circuit Court as if the original trial had not occurred.
appeals.bond_amount_notes: The bond amount is typically equal to the judgment amount plus court costs.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: Default judgments can be appealed within the same 10-day window.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on the judgment amount until paid in full.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, serve the bank, and the bank freezes and transfers funds to the court.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor maintains significant bank balances.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File a lien with the county recorder's office; the lien must be satisfied before the property can be sold or refinanced.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns real property with equity.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell debtor's non-exempt personal property.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution, sheriff seizes property, and it is sold at public auction to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt personal property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before the 10-year expiration; a hearing may be required.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are filed.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can appear without an attorney in General Sessions Court.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a trade name must file an Assumed Name Certificate with the county.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors can send a designated employee to represent them.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from recovering fees for work requiring a license.

county_variations.detail: Davidson County offers online filing through the Odyssey eFileTN system.
Shelby County has higher filing fees compared to other counties; check with the local clerk for current rates.

pitfalls.title: Wrong defendant naming
pitfalls.what_happens: If you sue 'John's Garage' but the legal entity is 'JG Auto Repair LLC', the judgment may be unenforceable against the LLC's assets.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Search the Secretary of State business records before filing and use the exact registered name; for sole proprietors use the individual's full legal name.

pitfalls.title: Missed statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing one day after the deadline gets the case dismissed with prejudice.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Check the SOL for your specific claim type before filing; for fraud, the discovery rule applies but be prepared to prove when you reasonably could have discovered it.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant isn't properly served, the case may be dismissed or delayed.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Tennessee's rules for service carefully; consider using the sheriff's office or a professional process server.

pitfalls.title: Failing to appear in court
pitfalls.what_happens: If you don't show up, the court may dismiss your case or enter a default judgment against you.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Mark your calendar and set reminders; if you can't attend, request a continuance in advance.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Without enough evidence, the judge may rule against you.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements before the hearing.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring court orders
pitfalls.what_happens: Failing to comply with court orders can result in contempt charges or dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Read and follow all court orders carefully; if unsure, seek legal advice.

recent_changes.title: Gibson County General Sessions Judge Helps Students Realize Choices Have Consequences
recent_changes.description: Judge Bradley Owens initiated a program to educate students on the legal system and the consequences of their choices.

recent_changes.title: Judge Ross Presented with Sharon G. Lee Award of Excellence
recent_changes.description: Judge James Y. Ross received the award for his contributions to the judiciary and community.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in General Sessions Court; individuals and businesses can represent themselves.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The timeline varies by county and case complexity, but most cases are resolved within 30 to 90 days.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in General Sessions Court is $25,000.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a General Sessions Court decision to Circuit Court within 10 days.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment is valid for 10 years and can be renewed before it expires.

state_specific_notes: Tennessee allows driver's license suspension as a collection tool for unpaid vehicle judgments.
General Sessions Courts have jurisdiction that varies by county, so local rules may differ.
E-filing availability varies by county; check with the local court clerk.
Some counties offer on-site mediation services to help resolve disputes before trial.
Tennessee's homestead exemption protects up to $5,000 of equity in a debtor's primary residence.

interesting_findings: Some General Sessions Courts in Tennessee offer night court sessions to accommodate working individuals.
Tennessee's Consumer Protection Act allows for treble damages in cases of willful violations.
Certain counties have specialized General Sessions Courts for environmental or housing cases.
Tennessee law permits the use of private process servers, providing flexibility in serving legal documents.
The state has a unique provision allowing for the suspension of professional licenses for non-payment of child support.

sources.label: TN AOC Court Costs and Fees
